54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74

76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. 141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147

If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".

152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. 43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.

176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	\begin{lemma}
185	
186	\leanok
187	  [Smooth \(\Rightarrow\) DVR at every codim-\(1\) point]
188	  \label{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing_dvr_of_codim_one}
190	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (the definition ``regular in codimension one'',
191	  % together with the immediately preceding remark that on a nonsingular variety every
192	  % local ring is regular) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
193	  % PDF page 147).
194	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textbf{Definition.} We say a scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in
195	  % codimension one} (or sometimes \emph{nonsingular in codimension one}) if every
196	  % local ring \(\mathcal O_x\) of \(X\) of dimension one is regular."
197	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The most important examples of such schemes are nonsingular
198	  % varieties over a field (I, \S5) and noetherian normal schemes. On a nonsingular
199	  % variety the local ring of every closed point is regular (I, 5.1), hence all the
200	  % local rings are regular, since they are localizations of the local rings of
201	  % closed points."
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
203	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
204	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\)."
205	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of regular in codimension one;
206	  the DVR consequence at a codim-1 point).}
207	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\) and let \(\eta \in X\) be a point with
208	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a
209	  discrete valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\).
210	\end{lemma}
211	
212	\begin{proof}
213	
214	\leanok
215	  By smoothness, \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular for every closed point \(x \in X\)
216	  (Hartshorne I.5.1). Localising a regular local ring at a prime ideal yields a regular
217	  local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
218	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular
219	  of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a
220	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
221	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
222	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the
223	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).

229	  \texttt{[Smooth\,(\dots)\,X.hom]\ [IsIntegral X.left]} and the hypothesis

239	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
240	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
241	
242	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
243	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
244	point.
245	
246	\begin{theorem}
247	
248	\leanok
249	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
250	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
252	  
253	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
254	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
255	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
256	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
257	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
258	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
259	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
260	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
261	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
262	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
263	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
264	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
265	
266	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
267	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
268	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
269	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
270	  in \(X\).
271	\end{theorem}
272

274	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
275	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
276	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
277	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from

279	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image

283	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
284	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by

287	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
288	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
289	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
290	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
291	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
292	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
293	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
294	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
295	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
296	% which is a contradiction."

298	  
299	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
300	
301	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
302	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
303	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
304	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
305	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
306	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
307	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
308	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
309	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
310	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
311	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
312	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
313	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
314	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
315	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
316	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
317	
318	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
319	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
320	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
321	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
322	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
323	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
324	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
325	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
326	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
327	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
328	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
329	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
330	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
331	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
332	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
333	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
334	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
335	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
336	
337	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
338	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
339	  agree on all of \(X\).
340	\end{proof}

342	\begin{remark}
343	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
344	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
345	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
346	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
347	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
348	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
349	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
350	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
351	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
352	
353	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
354	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
355	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
356	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
357	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
358	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
359	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
360	\end{remark}

423	%   \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x)\} \cup \{0\}$.
424	% Suppose \(\phi\) is not defined at \(x\). Then for some $f \in \operatorname{Im}
425	% (\varphi^\ast)\(, \)(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\(, and clearly \)\Phi$ is
426	% not defined at the points \((y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\).
427	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in \(\Delta\) (AG 9.2), and when we identify
428	% it with a subset of \(V\), it is a subset of \(V\) of codimension one passing through
429	% \(x\) on which \(\varphi\) is not defined."
430	\begin{proof}
431	  
432	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
433	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
434	
435	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
436	  \[
437	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
438	    \qquad
439	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
440	  \]
441	  via the composition
442	  \[
443	    U \times U
444	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
445	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
446	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
447	  \]
448	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
449	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
450	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
451	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).
452	
453	  \emph{Sub-step 2: \(\Phi\)-defined at \((x, x)\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\varphi\)-defined at
454	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
455	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
456	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
457	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
458	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
459	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
460	  identity
461	  \[
462	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
463	    \qquad u \in W',
464	  \]
465	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence $x \in
466	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
467	
468	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
469	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
470	  \(K(X \times X)\):
471	  \[
472	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
473	  \]
474	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
475	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
476	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
477	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
478	
479	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
480	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
481	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
482	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
483	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
484	  \[
485	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
486	  \]
487	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
488	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
489	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
490	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
491	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
492	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
493	  \[
494	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
495	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
496	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
497	  \]
498	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
499	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
500	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
501	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
502	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
503	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
504	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
505	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
506	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
507	
508	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
509	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
510	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
511	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
512	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
513	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
514	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
515	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
516	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
517	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
518	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
519	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
520	\end{proof}
521	
522	\begin{remark}
523	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
524	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
525	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
526	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
527	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
528	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
529	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
530	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
531	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
532	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
533	\end{remark}

535	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
536	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
537	
538	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
539	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
540	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
541	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
542	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
543	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
544	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
545	
546	\begin{theorem}
547	
548	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
549	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
550	  
551	  % NOTE (iter-179 review, lvb-checker `codimone-iter179` corrective):
552	  % `\lean{...}` pin DETACHED (Path D2 option (b) per the lvb-checker). The
553	  % substantive Hartshorne-II.6 valuative reformulation (the iff with
554	  % ord_W >= 0) below is the *intended* statement; the iter-179 Lane D
555	  % `mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point` decl is only a 2-LOC
556	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain` reshuffle that does NOT prove the
557	  % ord_W content, so re-pinning `\lean{...}` to it would falsely claim
558	  % kernel-clean formalization of the substantive Hartshorne iff. The
559	  % weaker reshuffle proven this iter will be pinned to its own light
560	  % `\begin{lemma}` block (working title `lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle`)
561	  % by an iter-180+ blueprint-writer dispatch; the substantive Hartshorne
562	  % version stays pinned here but unlinked until a future
563	  % `extend_iff_pullback_order_nonneg` lemma carries
564	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.order` machinery (pullback `RationalMap →
565	  % function-field ring map`, not yet in Mathlib at b80f227). See
566	  % task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-codimone-iter179.md +
567	  % proof-journal/sessions/session_179/recommendations.md REC-14.
568	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, pp.~130--131 (the valuation \(v_Y\) associated to a
569	  % prime divisor, and the characterisation of \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_Y}\) as the
570	  % subring of \(K(X)\) on which \(v_Y \geq 0\)) (read from
571	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
573	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
574	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\). We call the
575	  % corresponding discrete valuation \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
576	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function on \(X\).
577	  % Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has a \emph{zero} along
578	  % \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\) has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of
579	  % order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
580	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) and the order map).}
581	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a variety over \(\bar k\),
582	  let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map, and let \(W \subseteq X\) be a
583	  prime divisor with generic point \(\eta_W \in W\) (so
584	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1\)). Let \((U, \varphi_U)\) be any representative of
585	  \(f\) with \(U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset\). The following are equivalent:
586	  \begin{enumerate}
587	    \item \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) (i.e.\ \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), equivalently
588	      \(W \not\subseteq Z(f)\)).
589	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood \(V \subseteq Y\) of
590	      the generic image of \(f\) near \(W\), every regular function $g \in
591	      \mathcal O_Y(V)\( pulls back to a rational function \)\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
592	      satisfying
593	      \[
594	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
595	      \]
596	  \end{enumerate}
597	
598	  Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
599	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
600	  \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback
601	  \(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\) has \(\ord_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).
602	\end{theorem}
603	
604	\begin{proof}
605	  
606	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\) is a
607	  DVR with fraction field \(K(X)\) and uniformiser determined by \(W\); by
608	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly \(\ord_W\). The local ring at
609	  \(\eta_W\) is therefore characterised inside \(K(X)\) as the subring
610	  \[
611	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
612	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
613	  \]
614	  The rational map \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) if and only if, for any affine open
615	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\),
616	  the pullback \(\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)\) lands in
617	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\): this is the standard local characterisation of where a
618	  rational map is regular at a codim-\(1\) point of a smooth variety (it is the
619	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
620	  equivalent to \(\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0\) for every \(g \in A\), which is
621	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~\(\Leftrightarrow\)~(2) is therefore established.
622	
623	  For the consequence, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor \(W\) is
624	  contained in \(Z(f)\), which by the equivalence above is iff the \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
625	  condition holds for every prime divisor \(W\).
626	\end{proof}
627	
628	\begin{remark}
629	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
630	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
631	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
632	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
633	  isolation, the abstract codim-\(\geq 2\) statement
634	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
635	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
636	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
637	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-\(0\) classifying-map
638	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
639	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
640	\end{remark}

773	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve.} The proof of
774	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
775	    smooth surface \(X \times X\) but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
776	    \cref{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-\(1\)
777	    decomposition \(\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty\) on a nonsingular surface is
778	    used. The bilinear degree formula on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve (intersection
779	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
780	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
781	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
782	    introduced.
783	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
784	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout; descent of the
785	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
786	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
787	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
788	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
789	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and
790	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for \(G\) a group variety is sharper than any
791	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original \(X\)
792	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
793	\end{itemize}

38691	where $\overline{x}_1, \ldots, \overline{x}_r$ is a transcendence basis
38692	of $\kappa$ over $k$ and $\overline{y}$ is separable algebraic over
38693	$k(\overline{x}_1, \ldots, \overline{x}_r)$. Let $P(T)$ in
38694	$k(\overline{x}_1, \ldots, \overline{x}_r)[T]$ be the minimal
38695	polynomial of $\overline{y}$ over $k(\overline{x}_1, \ldots, \overline{x}_r)$.
38696	Then $P(\overline{y}) = 0$ but $P'(\overline{y}) \not = 0$. Choose any lifts
38697	$x_i \in R$ of the elements $\overline{x}_i \in \kappa$.
38698	This gives a commutative diagram
38699	$$
38700	\xymatrix{
38701	R \ar[r] & \kappa \\
38702	& k(\overline{x}_1, \ldots, \overline{x}_r) \ar[lu]^\varphi \ar[u]
38703	}
38704	$$
38705	of $k$-algebras. We want to extend the left upwards arrow
38706	$\varphi$ to a $k$-algebra
38707	map from $\kappa$ to $R$. To do this choose any $y \in R$ lifting
38708	$\overline{y}$. To see that it defines a $k$-algebra map
38709	defined on $\kappa \cong k(\overline{x}_1, \ldots, \overline{x}_r)[T]/(P)$

37100	Any localization $S_g$ is smooth over $R$.
37101	If $f \in R$ maps to an invertible element of $S$,
37102	then $R_f \to S$ is smooth.
37103	\end{lemma}
37104	
37105	\begin{proof}
37106	By Lemma \ref{lemma-localize-NL} the naive cotangent
37107	complex for $S_g$ over $R$ is the base change of the naive cotangent
37108	complex of $S$ over $R$. The assumption is that the naive cotangent
37109	complex of $S/R$ is $\Omega_{S/R}$ and that this is a finite projective
37110	$S$-module. Hence so is its base change. Thus $S_g$ is smooth over $R$.
37111	
37112	\medskip\noindent
37113	The second assertion follows in the same way from
37114	Lemma \ref{lemma-NL-localize-bottom}.
37115	\end{proof}
37116	
37117	\begin{lemma}
37118	\label{lemma-base-change-smooth}
37119	\begin{slogan}
37120	Smoothness is preserved under base change
37121	\end{slogan}
37122	Let $R \to S$ be a smooth ring map.
37123	Let $R \to R'$ be any ring map.
37124	Then the base change $R' \to S' = R' \otimes_R S$ is smooth.
37125	\end{lemma}
37126	
37127	\begin{proof}
37128	Let $\alpha : R[x_1, \ldots, x_n] \to S$ be a presentation
37129	with kernel $I$. Let $\alpha' : R'[x_1, \ldots, x_n] \to R' \otimes_R S$
37130	be the induced presentation. Let $I' = \Ker(\alpha')$.
37131	Since $0 \to I \to R[x_1, \ldots, x_n] \to S \to 0$
37132	is exact, the sequence
37133	$R' \otimes_R I \to R'[x_1, \ldots, x_n] \to R' \otimes_R S \to 0$
37134	is exact. Thus $R' \otimes_R I \to I'$ is surjective.
37135	By Definition \ref{definition-smooth} there is a short exact sequence
37136	$$
37137	0 \to I/I^2 \to
37138	\Omega_{R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/R} \otimes_{R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]} S \to
37139	\Omega_{S/R} \to
37140	0
37141	$$
37142	and the $S$-module $\Omega_{S/R}$ is finite projective.
37143	In particular $I/I^2$ is a direct summand of
37144	$\Omega_{R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/R} \otimes_{R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]} S$.
37145	Consider the commutative diagram
37146	$$
37147	\xymatrix{
37148	R' \otimes_R (I/I^2) \ar[r] \ar[d] &
37149	R' \otimes_R (\Omega_{R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/R} \otimes_{R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]} S)
37150	\ar[d] \\
37151	I'/(I')^2 \ar[r] &
37152	\Omega_{R'[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/R'}
37153	\otimes_{R'[x_1, \ldots, x_n]} (R' \otimes_R S)
37154	}
37155	$$
37156	Since the right vertical map is an isomorphism we see that
37157	the left vertical map is injective and surjective by what was
37158	said above. Thus we conclude that $\NL(\alpha')$ is quasi-isomorphic
37159	to $\Omega_{S'/R'} \cong S' \otimes_S \Omega_{S/R}$ placed in degree $0$.
37160	This module is finite projective since it is the base change
37161	of a finite projective module.
37162	\end{proof}
37163	
37164	\begin{lemma}
37165	\label{lemma-smooth-over-field}
37166	Let $k$ be a field.
37167	Let $S$ be a smooth $k$-algebra.
37168	Then $S$ is a local complete intersection.
37169	\end{lemma}
37170	
37171	\begin{proof}
37172	By Lemmas \ref{lemma-base-change-smooth} and
37173	\ref{lemma-lci-field-change} it suffices to prove this when
37174	$k$ is algebraically closed. Choose a presentation
37175	$\alpha : k[x_1, \ldots, x_n] \to S$ with kernel $I$. Let $\mathfrak m$
37176	be a maximal ideal of $S$, and let $\mathfrak m' \supset I$ be the
37177	corresponding maximal ideal of $k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]$.
37178	We will show that condition (5) of
37179	Lemma \ref{lemma-lci}
37180	holds (with $\mathfrak m$ instead of $\mathfrak q$).
37181	We may write $\mathfrak m' = (x_1 - a_1, \ldots, x_n - a_n)$
37182	for some $a_i \in k$, because $k$ is algebraically closed, see
37183	Theorem \ref{theorem-nullstellensatz}.
37184	By our assumption that $k \to S$ is smooth the $S$-module map
37185	$\text{d} : I/I^2 \to \bigoplus_{i = 1}^n S \text{d}x_i$
37186	is a split injection. Hence the corresponding map
37187	$I/\mathfrak m' I \to \bigoplus \kappa(\mathfrak m') \text{d}x_i$
37188	is injective. Say $\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak m')}(I/\mathfrak m' I) = c$
37189	and pick $f_1, \ldots, f_c \in I$ which map to a $\kappa(\mathfrak m')$-basis
37190	of $I/\mathfrak m' I$. By
37191	Nakayama's Lemma \ref{lemma-NAK}
37192	we see that $f_1, \ldots, f_c$ generate $I_{\mathfrak m'}$ over
37193	$k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]_{\mathfrak m'}$. Consider the commutative diagram
37194	$$
37195	\xymatrix{
37196	I \ar[r] \ar[d] & I/I^2 \ar[rr] \ar[d] & &
37197	I/\mathfrak m'I \ar[d] \\
37198	\Omega_{k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/k} \ar[r] &
37199	\bigoplus S\text{d}x_i \ar[rr]^{\text{d}x_i \mapsto x_i - a_i} & &
37200	\mathfrak m'/(\mathfrak m')^2
37201	}
37202	$$
37203	(proof commutativity omitted). The middle vertical map is the one defining
37204	the naive cotangent complex of $\alpha$. Note that the right lower
37205	horizontal arrow induces an isomorphism
37206	$\bigoplus \kappa(\mathfrak m') \text{d}x_i \to \mathfrak m'/(\mathfrak m')^2$.
37207	Hence our generators $f_1, \ldots, f_c$ of $I_{\mathfrak m'}$ map to a
37208	collection of elements in $k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]_{\mathfrak m'}$ whose
37209	classes in $\mathfrak m'/(\mathfrak m')^2$ are linearly independent
37210	over $\kappa(\mathfrak m')$. Therefore they form a regular sequence
37211	in the ring $k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]_{\mathfrak m'}$ by
37212	Lemma \ref{lemma-regular-ring-CM}.
37213	This verifies condition (5) of
37214	Lemma \ref{lemma-lci}
37215	hence $S_g$ is a global complete intersection over $k$ for some
37216	$g \in S$, $g \not \in \mathfrak m$. As this works for any maximal
37217	ideal of $S$ we conclude that $S$ is a local complete intersection over $k$.
37218	\end{proof}
37219	
37220	\begin{definition}
37221	\label{definition-standard-smooth}
37222	Let $R$ be a ring. Given integers $n \geq c \geq 0$ and
37223	$f_1, \ldots, f_c \in R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]$ we say
37224	$$
37225	R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/(f_1, \ldots, f_c)
37226	$$
37227	is a {\it standard smooth algebra over $R$} if the polynomial
37228	$$
37229	g =
37230	\det
37231	\left(
37232	\begin{matrix}
37233	\partial f_1/\partial x_1 &
37234	\partial f_2/\partial x_1 &
37235	\ldots &
37236	\partial f_c/\partial x_1 \\
37237	\partial f_1/\partial x_2 &
37238	\partial f_2/\partial x_2 &
37239	\ldots &
37240	\partial f_c/\partial x_2 \\
37241	\ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots \\
37242	\partial f_1/\partial x_c &
37243	\partial f_2/\partial x_c &
37244	\ldots &
37245	\partial f_c/\partial x_c
37246	\end{matrix}
37247	\right)
37248	$$
37249	maps to an invertible element in $R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/(f_1, \ldots, f_c)$.
37250	We say an $R$-algebra $S$ is {\it standard smooth} or that
37251	the ring map $R \to S$ is {\it standard smooth} if there exist
37252	$n \geq c \geq 0$ and $f_1, \ldots, f_c \in R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]$
37253	such that $R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/(f_1, \ldots, f_c)$ is a standard smooth
37254	algebra over $R$ and $S$ is isomorphic to
37255	$R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/(f_1, \ldots, f_c)$ as an $R$-algebra.
37256	\end{definition}
37257	
37258	\begin{lemma}
37259	\label{lemma-standard-smooth}
37260	Let
37261	$S = R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/(f_1, \ldots, f_c) = R[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/I$
37262	be a standard smooth algebra. Then
37263	\begin{enumerate}
37264	\item the ring map $R \to S$ is smooth,
37265	\item the $S$-module $\Omega_{S/R}$ is free on
37266	$\text{d}x_{c + 1}, \ldots, \text{d}x_n$,
37267	\item the $S$-module $I/I^2$ is free on the classes of $f_1, \ldots, f_c$,
37268	\item for any $g \in S$ the ring map $R \to S_g$ is standard smooth,
37269	\item for any ring map $R \to R'$ the base change
37270	$R' \to R'\otimes_R S$ is standard smooth,
37271	\item if $f \in R$ maps to an invertible element in $S$, then
37272	$R_f \to S$ is standard smooth, and
37273	\item the ring $S$ is a relative global complete intersection over $R$.
37274	\end{enumerate}
37275	\end{lemma}
37276	
37277	\begin{proof}
37278	Consider the naive cotangent complex of the given presentation
37279	$$
37280	(f_1, \ldots, f_c)/(f_1, \ldots, f_c)^2
37281	\longrightarrow
37282	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i = 1}^n S \text{d}x_i
37283	$$
37284	Let us compose this map with the projection onto the first $c$ direct summands
37285	of the direct sum. According to the definition of a standard smooth
37286	algebra the classes $f_i \bmod (f_1, \ldots, f_c)^2$ map to a basis of
37287	$\bigoplus_{i = 1}^c S\text{d}x_i$. We conclude that
37288	$(f_1, \ldots, f_c)/(f_1, \ldots, f_c)^2$ is free of rank $c$ with
37289	a basis given by the elements $f_i \bmod (f_1, \ldots, f_c)^2$, and
37290	that the homology in degree $0$, i.e., $\Omega_{S/R}$,
37291	of the naive cotangent complex is a free $S$-module with basis the images of
37292	$\text{d}x_{c + j}$, $j = 1, \ldots, n - c$.
37293	In particular, this proves $R \to S$ is smooth.
37294	
37295	\medskip\noindent
37296	The proofs of (4) and (6) are omitted. But see the example below and
37297	the proof of
37298	Lemma \ref{lemma-base-change-relative-global-complete-intersection}.
37299

Lemma 10.140.3. Let $k$ be any field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$-algebra. Let $X = \mathop{\mathrm{Spec}}(S)$. Let $\mathfrak q \subset S$ be a prime corresponding to $x \in X$. The following are equivalent:

  1. The $k$-algebra $S$ is smooth at $\mathfrak q$ over $k$.

  2. We have $\dim _{\kappa (\mathfrak q)} \Omega _{S/k} \otimes _ S \kappa (\mathfrak q) \leq \dim _ x X$.

  3. We have $\dim _{\kappa (\mathfrak q)} \Omega _{S/k} \otimes _ S \kappa (\mathfrak q) = \dim _ x X$.

Moreover, in this case the local ring $S_{\mathfrak q}$ is regular.

Proof. If $S$ is smooth at $\mathfrak q$ over $k$, then there exists a $g \in S$, $g \not\in \mathfrak q$ such that $S_ g$ is standard smooth over $k$, see Lemma 10.137.9. A standard smooth algebra over $k$ has a module of differentials which is free of rank equal to the dimension, see Lemma 10.137.6 (use that a relative global complete intersection over a field has dimension equal to the number of variables minus the number of equations). Thus we see that (1) implies (3). To finish the proof of the lemma it suffices to show that (2) implies (1) and that it implies that $S_{\mathfrak q}$ is regular.

Assume (2). By Nakayama's Lemma 10.20.1 we see that $\Omega _{S/k, \mathfrak q}$ can be generated by $\leq \dim _ x X$ elements. We may replace $S$ by $S_ g$ for some $g \in S$, $g \not\in \mathfrak q$ such that $\Omega _{S/k}$ is generated by at most $\dim _ x X$ elements. Let $K/k$ be an algebraically closed field extension such that there exists a $k$-algebra map $\psi : \kappa (\mathfrak q) \to K$. Consider $S_ K = K \otimes _ k S$. Let $\mathfrak m \subset S_ K$ be the maximal ideal corresponding to the surjection

\[ \xymatrix{ S_ K = K \otimes _ k S \ar[r] & K \otimes _ k \kappa (\mathfrak q) \ar[r]^-{\text{id}_ K \otimes \psi } & K. } \]

Note that $\mathfrak m \cap S = \mathfrak q$, in other words $\mathfrak m$ lies over $\mathfrak q$. By Lemma 10.116.6 the dimension of $X_ K = \mathop{\mathrm{Spec}}(S_ K)$ at the point corresponding to $\mathfrak m$ is $\dim _ x X$. By Lemma 10.114.6 this is equal to $\dim ((S_ K)_{\mathfrak m})$. By Lemma 10.131.12 the module of differentials of $S_ K$ over $K$ is the base change of $\Omega _{S/k}$, hence also generated by at most $\dim _ x X = \dim ((S_ K)_{\mathfrak m})$ elements. By Lemma 10.140.2 we see that $S_ K$ is smooth at $\mathfrak m$ over $K$. By Lemma 10.137.17 this implies that $S$ is smooth at $\mathfrak q$ over $k$. This proves (1). Moreover, we know by Lemma 10.140.2 that the local ring $(S_ K)_{\mathfrak m}$ is regular. Since $S_{\mathfrak q} \to (S_ K)_{\mathfrak m}$ is flat we conclude from Lemma 10.110.9 that $S_{\mathfrak q}$ is regular.

3	\chapter{Coheight--Krull dim bridge for scheme points}
4	\label{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}
5	
6	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-183). This chapter is a project-side scaffold sitting
7	% between \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and the Riemann--Roch
8	% Weil-divisor machinery of \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}. The
9	% directive originates from the iter-182 plan-phase api-alignment consult
10	% \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} (Decision 2), which determined
11	% that Mathlib at the project's pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) ships every
12	% prerequisite for the scheme-level coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk
13	% identification but does \emph{not} ship the bridge itself. The bridge is
14	% \emph{not} a published-textbook lemma; it is the project-side assembly of
15	% four standard Mathlib API pieces:
16	%   1. \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} -- coheight transport across order
17	%      isomorphisms (Mathlib \texttt{Order.KrullDimension}).
18	%   2. \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} -- opens contain
19	%      every generisation of their points (Mathlib \texttt{Topology.Inseparable}).
20	%   3. \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} -- the algebra
21	%      bridge between localisation Krull dim and prime-ideal height
22	%      (Mathlib \texttt{RingTheory.Ideal.Height}).
23	%   4. \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk} -- the affine-stalk
24	%      \(=\) localisation identification (Mathlib \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).
25	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}
26	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
27	% creating it. None of the four blocks below has a direct Hartshorne / Milne /
28	% Mumford / Stacks tag that quotes the exact statement, so the blocks are
29	% packaged as Archon-original assembly lemmas with explicit Mathlib API
30	% pointers in lieu of \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE:} blocks.
31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point \(z\) of a scheme \(X\) has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at \(z\)}, written
37	\(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}\), is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\). The
39	\emph{coheight} of \(z\) in \(X\), written \(\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)\), is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of \(X\): the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in \(X\), where \(\prec\) is the
43	strict specialisation order \(x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x\).
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]
49	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
50	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
51	statement that the dimension of the local ring at \(z\) counts irreducible
52	closed subsets of \(X\) containing \(z\) --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
53	commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
54	and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.
55	
56	\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
57	currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
58	\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
59	codim-\(1\) use site:
60	\begin{itemize}
61	  \item \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} carries an internal conjunction
62	    \[
63	      \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing\ } \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;\wedge\;
64	      \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1
65	    \]
66	    inside the private helper
67	    \texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot};
68	    the Krull-dim conjunct is supplied by \emph{this chapter's}
69	    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}, leaving only the
70	    irreducible \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} half (which is the separate

\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
\label{sec:smooth_dvr}

The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.

The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.

\begin{lemma}
  [Smooth over \(\bar k\) \(\Rightarrow\) regular local stalk (Stacks 00TT)]
  \label{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT --- Jacobian criterion
  % (smooth-implies-regular direction) (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex,
  % L38593--38611; tag verified against the Stacks website tag/00TT page,
  % "Lemma 10.140.3" in section 10.140 "Smooth algebras over fields").
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be any field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$-algebra.
  % Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak q \subset S$ be a prime corresponding to
  % $x \in X$. The following are equivalent:
  % \begin{enumerate}
  % \item The $k$-algebra $S$ is smooth at $\mathfrak q$ over $k$.
  % \item We have
  % $\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak q)
  % \leq \dim_x X$.
  % \item We have
  % $\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak q)
  % = \dim_x X$.
  % \end{enumerate}
  % Moreover, in this case the local ring $S_{\mathfrak q}$ is regular."
  % NOTE (iter-185 writer): the `\uses{...}` line is intentionally empty.
  % The Stacks 00TT prerequisites (smooth ring map, regular local ring) live
  % in Mathlib as typeclasses (`Algebra.Smooth`, `IsRegularLocalRing`) but are
  % not packaged as blueprint-labeled definitions in this project; the project's
  % `def:codim_one_indeterminacy` and Auslander--Buchsbaum-side
  % `cor:regular_cohen_macaulay` are adjacent context, not strict prerequisites.
  \uses{}
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT.}
  Let \(X\) be a variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) (geometrically
  integral, separated, locally of finite type) and assume the structure morphism
  \(X \to \Spec(\bar k)\) is \emph{smooth}. Then for every point \(z \in X\) the
  stalk \(\mathcal O_{X, z}\) is a regular local ring.

  The hypothesis \([IsAlgClosed\,\bar k]\) is load-bearing: tag 00TT is stated
  over an arbitrary field, but the variant Mathlib will be able to discharge
  axiom-clean at commit \texttt{b80f227} routes through residue fields that are
  \(\bar k\)-rational (every closed point of \(X\) has residue field \(\bar k\) under
  algebraic closure, and the chain of localisations from a closed point reaches
  every \(z \in X\) by the standard chain-of-primes argument). Without algebraic
  closure one needs the geometric-regularity refinement of tag 00TT (Stacks tag
  \texttt{056T} in the project's \texttt{stacks-varieties.md} card) and an extra
  base-change-to-\(\bar k\) step.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT.} The cited lemma packages the
  Jacobian-criterion equivalence and concludes ``in this case the local ring
  \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is regular''. Translated to the scheme level: a smooth
  finite-type morphism \(X \to \Spec(\bar k)\) restricted to an affine chart
  \(\Spec(S) \subseteq X\) gives an affine algebra \(\bar k \to S\) that is
  smooth at every prime \(\mathfrak q \subset S\); tag 00TT then yields
  regularity of the localisation \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) at every
  point \(z \in X\) lying over \(\mathfrak q\).

  Mathlib hooks the prover will assemble for the formalised version of this
  block at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (i) \texttt{Algebra.FormallySmooth} and the
  smooth-locus-of-a-scheme typeclass instance threading
  \texttt{[Smooth\,X.hom]} down to the affine algebra
  \(\bar k \to \mathcal O_X(U)\) on each open affine \(U \subseteq X\); (ii) the
  Jacobian-criterion refinement of \texttt{Module.FormallyEtale} /
  \texttt{Algebra.Smooth} via the cotangent complex \texttt{H\^{}1\,LSm} (or
  equivalent), as encoded in Mathlib's \texttt{Algebra.Smooth.iff} family; (iii)
  the closed-point selection that, given a non-closed \(z\), picks a closed
  specialisation \(x \rightsquigarrow z\) whose stalk localises to
  \(\mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.localRing\_at\_closed\_point}
  family); (iv) preservation of regularity under localisation of a regular
  local ring at a prime (Mathlib
  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.localization\_isRegularLocalRing}, or the same
  fact phrased via Stacks tag \texttt{00OF}). The composition of (i)--(iv) is
  the formalised content of the smooth-implies-regular implication; the only
  Mathlib gap at \texttt{b80f227} is in step (ii)--(iii), specifically the
  Jacobian-criterion scheme-level packaging that promotes
  \texttt{[Smooth\,X.hom]\,[IsAlgClosed\,\bar k]} to
  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing\,(X.left.presheaf.stalk\,z)}.

  As a backup citation, Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Chapter~19
  (``Regular Rings'') develops the regular-local-ring theory needed for
  step~(iv); see \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} for the depth-/CM-side
  references the project already maintains.
\end{proof}
